topics.title: Concurrency & Threads in Java
topics.description: Java concurrency covers creating and managing threads, coordinating shared state safely, and composing asynchronous work. The core challenge is that multiple threads accessing shared mutable state without coordination leads to race conditions, visibility bugs, and deadlocks. Java provides three layers: low-level (synchronized, volatile, wait/notify), mid-level (ReentrantLock, Semaphore, CountDownLatch), and high-level (ExecutorService, CompletableFuture, ForkJoinPool). Modern Java favours the high-level APIs and immutability to avoid concurrency bugs entirely.
topics.story: Think of concurrency like a busy restaurant kitchen. Threads are chefs. Shared data is the prep counter. Without coordination, two chefs grab the same ingredient simultaneously — one gets nothing (race condition). synchronized is like a single key to the prep counter — only one chef at a time. volatile is like a whiteboard visible to all chefs — they always read the latest order, not their cached memory. ReentrantLock is a fancier key with a timer (tryLock). ExecutorService is the head chef who assigns tasks from a queue to available sous-chefs (thread pool). CompletableFuture is a ticket system — place an order, get a ticket, collect the result when ready.
topics.analogy: Thread safety = a shared Google Doc. Without synchronisation, two people typing simultaneously causes lost updates (race). synchronized = 'Lock for editing' button — one editor at a time. volatile = 'Show all changes in real time' — no stale cached view. ReentrantLock = the editor can lock multiple sections and release each independently. CompletableFuture = 'Suggest edit' — the change is proposed asynchronously and applied when approved (chained).
topics.memoryAnchor: VITAL checklist — Visibility (volatile fixes stale CPU cache reads; synchronized fixes both visibility AND atomicity). Isolation (ThreadLocal gives each thread its own private copy). Thread-safe classes (ConcurrentHashMap, AtomicInteger, CopyOnWriteArrayList — use these instead of synchronized wrappers). Atomic operations needed for read-modify-write (i++ is NOT atomic — it is read+increment+write, 3 separate ops). Locks (ReentrantLock = flexible synchronized with tryLock/fairness). RACE mnemonic: Read-Act-Concurrently-Explodes. Deadlock prevention: ALWAYS acquire locks in the SAME ORDER across all threads. volatile = visibility only. synchronized = visibility + atomicity + mutual exclusion.
topics.firstPrinciples: CPUs cache values in registers and L1/L2 cache — without a memory barrier, a thread may never see updates written by another thread (visibility problem). The Java Memory Model (JMM) defines when writes become visible: synchronised exit, volatile write, thread start, and join all flush caches. Race condition = the correctness of the result depends on the relative timing of thread execution. The only safe approaches: (1) make the data immutable, (2) confine it to one thread, (3) synchronise all accesses.
topics.bruteForce: Without any concurrency control: int counter = 0; two threads each do counter++ 1000 times. Expected result: 2000. Actual result: anything from 1001 to 2000 because counter++ is three operations (read, increment, write) and threads interleave. Debugging is nearly impossible — the bug disappears under the debugger (Heisenbug). Manual Thread.sleep() for coordination is fragile and slow.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use AtomicInteger for simple counters (CAS — lock-free). Use ConcurrentHashMap for concurrent maps. Use BlockingQueue for producer-consumer patterns. Use ReentrantLock.tryLock() with timeout to avoid deadlocks. Use ExecutorService for thread lifecycle management. Use CompletableFuture for async pipelines. Use ForkJoinPool for divide-and-conquer parallel computation. Prefer immutable objects (records, unmodifiable collections) — they are always thread-safe.
topics.whenToUse: synchronized: simple critical sections where you own the object. volatile: single-writer, multiple-reader flag (e.g., shutdown flag). ReentrantLock: timeout-based locking, interruptible waiting, multiple Conditions on one lock. AtomicInteger/AtomicReference: simple counter or reference CAS operations. ExecutorService: managing a pool of threads for repeated tasks. CompletableFuture: async I/O chains, HTTP calls, parallel service aggregation. ForkJoinPool: recursive divide-and-conquer (parallel sort, parallel tree traversal). BlockingQueue: producer-consumer with backpressure.

examples.title: Race Condition, synchronized, and volatile
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":6,"lineCode":"static void unsafeIncrement() { unsafeCounter++; }","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"counter++ compiles to three bytecodes: GETFIELD (read), IADD (increment), PUTFIELD (write). Two threads can both GETFIELD=5, both compute 6, both PUTFIELD=6 — result is 6 not 7. This is the lost update race condition."},{"line":10,"lineCode":"static synchronized void syncIncrement() { syncCounter++; }","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"synchronized acquires the monitor lock on the class object before entering. Only one thread can hold it at a time. Guarantees atomicity AND visibility (lock release flushes CPU cache to main memory)."},{"line":12,"lineCode":"static AtomicInteger atomicCounter = new AtomicInteger(0);","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"AtomicInteger uses CAS (Compare-And-Swap) hardware instruction: if current value = expected, swap to new. No lock acquired. If CAS fails (another thread changed it), retry. Lock-free but not wait-free."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"static volatile boolean shutdownFlag = false;","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"volatile on a field: (1) reads always go to main memory — no thread cache. (2) writes immediately flush to main memory. Guarantees visibility for single read/write. Does NOT prevent race conditions for read-modify-write."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Race_Condition
      T1[Thread 1: read counter=5] --> T3[both write 6]
      T2[Thread 2: read counter=5] --> T3
      T3 --> LOST[Lost update: expected 6 got 6 not 7]
    end
    subgraph synchronized
      T4[Thread 1: acquire lock] --> T6[increment to 6]
      T5[Thread 2: BLOCKED] --> T7[wait for lock release]
      T6 --> T8[release lock]
      T8 --> T7
      T7 --> T9[increment to 7]
    end
    subgraph volatile
      W[Writer: shutdownFlag=true] -->|flush to main memory| M[Main Memory]
      M -->|always read from here| R[Reader: sees true immediately]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
RACE CONDITION: counter++ = read + modify + write (3 ops)
  interleaving: T1 reads 5, T2 reads 5, T1 writes 6, T2 writes 6 → lost update

synchronized method: JVM acquires monitor on 'this'
  only one thread inside at a time
  on exit: flushes all writes to main memory

AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet():
  CAS loop: if(current == expected) swap else retry
  lock-free, CPU-level atomic instruction

volatile field:
  read  → always from main memory (no CPU register cache)
  write → immediately to main memory
  NOT atomic for compound ops (counter++ still a race)
examples.explanation: counter++ is not atomic — it's three separate operations. Threads interleave between them causing lost updates. synchronized guarantees mutual exclusion and memory visibility. AtomicInteger uses hardware CAS — faster than synchronized for simple operations. volatile only guarantees visibility (no stale cached values) but NOT atomicity — never use volatile for counters, only for flags that are written by one thread and read by others.
examples.realWorldUse: Servlet containers (Tomcat) create one thread per request — any servlet field accessed concurrently must be thread-safe. Spring beans are singletons shared across threads — never store mutable per-request state in instance fields. AtomicInteger is used for request counters in metrics systems (Micrometer). volatile is used for shutdown flags in background threads and for double-checked locking patterns.

examples.title: ReentrantLock, Conditions, and Deadlock Prevention
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":18,"lineCode":"while (count == 0) notEmpty.await();","variables":{"count":0},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"await() atomically releases the lock AND suspends the thread. When signalled, re-acquires the lock before continuing. MUST be in a while loop (not if) — spurious wakeups can occur where await returns without a signal."},{"line":23,"lineCode":"notFull.signal();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"signal() wakes one thread waiting on the notFull condition. That thread will try to re-acquire the lock when the current thread releases it in finally. signalAll() wakes all waiters — needed when multiple threads wait on the same condition."},{"line":48,"lineCode":"Account first = from.id < to.id ? from : to;","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Deadlock occurs when T1 holds lockA waits lockB AND T2 holds lockB waits lockA. Prevention: always acquire locks in a canonical order (here: by account ID). Both threads will always try lock(lower_id) first — no circular wait possible."},{"line":51,"lineCode":"first.lock.lock();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"ReentrantLock is reentrant: the same thread can acquire it multiple times without deadlocking. tryLock(timeout) is also available — avoids blocking indefinitely, returns false if lock unavailable within timeout."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph BoundedBuffer
      P[Producer: put] -->|buffer full| W1[notFull.await release lock]
      W1 -->|consumer signals| A1[re-acquire lock continue]
      C[Consumer: take] -->|buffer empty| W2[notEmpty.await release lock]
      W2 -->|producer signals| A2[re-acquire lock continue]
    end
    subgraph DeadlockPrevention
      T1[T1: transfer A to B] -->|always lock lower ID first| L1[lock A id=1]
      L1 --> L2[lock B id=2]
      T2[T2: transfer B to A] -->|always lock lower ID first| L3[lock A id=1 wait]
      L2 --> Done[transfer done]
      Done --> R1[unlock B]
      R1 --> R2[unlock A]
      R2 --> L3
    end
examples.pseudocode:
REENTRANTLOCK vs synchronized:
  lock.lock()       acquire (blocking)
  lock.tryLock(t)   acquire with timeout — returns false if unavailable
  lock.unlock()     release — ALWAYS in finally block
  lock.newCondition() creates Condition for this lock

CONDITION (like wait/notify but per-condition):
  condition.await()   release lock, suspend thread
  condition.signal()  wake one waiter
  MUST: await() in while loop (spurious wakeups)

DEADLOCK PREVENTION:
  always acquire multiple locks in a fixed canonical order
  use tryLock with timeout as an alternative
  use one lock for multiple resources when possible
examples.explanation: ReentrantLock gives explicit lock/unlock control with features synchronized lacks: tryLock with timeout, interruptible lock acquisition, and multiple Condition objects per lock. await() on a Condition atomically releases the lock and suspends — avoiding the missed signal problem of Object.wait(). The deadlock prevention rule is universal: always acquire locks in the same total order across all threads. Lock in ID order eliminates circular wait — one of the four Coffman conditions for deadlock.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's LinkedBlockingQueue uses ReentrantLock with two Conditions (notEmpty, notFull) — exactly the pattern shown. Database connection pools (HikariCP) use ReentrantLock with tryLock for non-blocking connection acquisition with a timeout. Deadlock detection in production: jstack or VisualVM shows thread dumps where you can see BLOCKED threads and the lock ownership graph.

examples.title: ExecutorService and Thread Pools
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":14,"lineCode":"ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(4);","variables":{"coreThreads":4,"maxThreads":4},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Fixed pool: always 4 threads, unbounded task queue. Tasks above 4 concurrent wait in the queue. Never creates more than 4 threads regardless of queue depth."},{"line":18,"lineCode":"futures.add(pool.submit(() -> expensiveCompute(n)));","variables":{"n":1},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"submit() enqueues the task and returns immediately with a Future. The thread pool assigns available worker threads to tasks from the queue. Non-blocking — main thread continues to submit all 10 tasks quickly."},{"line":24,"lineCode":"for (Future<Integer> f : futures) results.add(f.get());","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"f.get() blocks until that specific future is complete. If we call get() in submission order, we might wait on f1 even if f3 is already done. For true parallel collection use CompletableFuture.allOf() instead."},{"line":52,"lineCode":"new ThreadPoolExecutor(2, 4, 60, SECONDS, new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(100), factory)","variables":{"core":2,"max":4,"queueCapacity":100},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"ThreadPoolExecutor rules: tasks 1-2 create core threads. Tasks 3+ go to queue. If queue full, create threads up to max (4). If max reached AND queue full, apply RejectedExecutionHandler (default: throw RejectedExecutionException)."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[submit task] --> B{core threads available?}
    B -->|yes| C[assign to core thread]
    B -->|no| D{queue has space?}
    D -->|yes| E[enqueue task]
    D -->|no| F{below max threads?}
    F -->|yes| G[create new thread]
    F -->|no| H[RejectedExecutionHandler]
    E --> I[core thread picks up task]
    G --> J[executes task, then idles]
    J -->|idle > keepAlive| K[thread dies if above core]
examples.pseudocode:
THREAD POOL TYPES:
  newFixedThreadPool(n)    → n threads, unbounded queue
  newCachedThreadPool()    → 0..∞ threads, 60s idle expire
  newSingleThreadExecutor()→ 1 thread, tasks in order
  newScheduledThreadPool(n)→ fixed pool + schedule/periodic

LIFECYCLE:
  submit(callable) → Future<T>   (non-blocking)
  future.get()     → T           (blocks until done)
  invokeAll(tasks) → List<Future> (blocks until all done)
  shutdown()       → stop new tasks, finish existing
  awaitTermination(t, unit) → wait up to t for shutdown

THREADPOOLEXECUTOR FLOW:
  task → core threads → bounded queue → max threads → reject
examples.explanation: ExecutorService decouples task submission from thread lifecycle. The pool reuses threads — no Thread.start() overhead per task. submit() is non-blocking; get() blocks until the result is ready. Proper shutdown requires shutdown() followed by awaitTermination() — without this, the JVM may not exit. ThreadPoolExecutor constructor gives full control: tune core/max threads and queue size to match CPU vs I/O workload.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring @Async uses a ThreadPoolTaskExecutor internally. Tomcat uses a ThreadPoolExecutor for HTTP request handling (default: 10 core, 200 max). Kafka consumer groups use single-thread executors per partition. Microservice pattern: one thread pool per external service call — if one service is slow, it only exhausts its own pool, not the shared pool (bulkhead pattern).

examples.title: CompletableFuture — Async Chaining
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":21,"lineCode":"CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchUser(42))","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"supplyAsync runs the supplier on ForkJoinPool.commonPool() asynchronously. Returns immediately with a CompletableFuture. The result is set when the lambda completes."},{"line":22,"lineCode":".thenApply(user -> fetchOrders(user))","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"thenApply transforms the result when it is available — like Stream.map() but for a future. Runs on the same thread pool. Returns a new CompletableFuture<List<String>>."},{"line":30,"lineCode":".thenCompose(user -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchOrders(user)))","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"thenCompose is flatMap for futures — the function returns a new CompletableFuture. Use when the next step is itself async. thenApply would give CF<CF<T>>; thenCompose gives CF<T>."},{"line":47,"lineCode":".exceptionally(ex -> \"fallback-user\")","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"exceptionally handles any exception in the chain — like catch in try-catch. The fallback value flows through subsequent thenApply calls normally. If no exception, exceptionally is skipped."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A[supplyAsync runs on pool] -->|result| B[thenApply transform]
    B -->|result| C[thenApply transform again]
    C -->|join| D[caller gets result]
    E[supplyAsync u1 parallel] --> G[allOf wait for all]
    F[supplyAsync u2 parallel] --> G
    H[supplyAsync u3 parallel] --> G
    G --> I[join all in ~50ms]
    J[supplyAsync throws] --> K[exceptionally fallback]
    K --> L[thenApply on fallback]
examples.pseudocode:
COMPLETABLEFUTURE METHODS:
  supplyAsync(supplier)     → CF<T>    run async, produce value
  thenApply(fn)             → CF<R>    transform result (like map)
  thenCompose(fn→CF)        → CF<R>    flatMap — fn returns CF
  thenCombine(other, fn)    → CF<R>    merge two CFs when both done
  allOf(cf1,cf2,cf3)        → CF<Void> wait for all
  anyOf(cf1,cf2)            → CF<Object> first to complete
  exceptionally(fn)         → CF<T>    handle exception, provide default
  handle(fn(T,ex))          → CF<R>    handle both result and exception
  join()                    → T        block and get (unchecked exception)
examples.explanation: CompletableFuture is a promise — a container for a value that will be available in the future. thenApply chains synchronous transforms (like map). thenCompose chains async steps without nesting (like flatMap). allOf runs futures in parallel and waits for all. exceptionally provides a fallback value on any exception in the chain, allowing error recovery without breaking the chain. join() blocks the calling thread until the result is available.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring WebFlux uses reactive streams (similar concept). REST API aggregation: fetch user, order, and inventory in parallel with allOf then combine. Retry pattern: supplyAsync → exceptionally → retrigger. Netflix circuit breaker: CompletableFuture.anyOf(serviceCall, timeoutFallback). Database async drivers (R2DBC, Vert.x) return CompletableFuture-compatible objects.

examples.title: ForkJoinPool — Parallel Divide and Conquer
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":17,"lineCode":"if (size <= THRESHOLD) { /* compute directly */ }","variables":{"size":1000,"threshold":1000},"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","annotation":"Base case: when the subarray is small enough (1000 elements), compute directly without splitting. Threshold choice is critical: too small = too many tasks, too large = no parallelism."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"left.fork();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"fork() asynchronously schedules the left subtask on the ForkJoinPool's deque. The current thread continues to compute the right subtask directly — this is the key fork-join pattern."},{"line":28,"lineCode":"long rightResult = right.compute();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Compute right subtask on the current thread while left runs on another thread in parallel. Computing one side directly avoids the overhead of fork+join for the other half."},{"line":29,"lineCode":"long leftResult = left.join();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"join() waits for the forked left task and retrieves its result. If the left task hasn't started yet (another thread stole it and it's queued), the current thread can help by executing it directly — this is work stealing."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[ParallelSum 0..10M] -->|fork| B[Left: 0..5M]
    A -->|compute| C[Right: 5M..10M]
    B -->|fork| D[Left: 0..2.5M]
    B -->|compute| E[Right: 2.5M..5M]
    D --> F[base case: sum 0..2.5M]
    E --> G[base case: sum 2.5M..5M]
    C --> H[base case: sum 5M..10M]
    F & G --> B_result[left result]
    B_result & H --> A_result[total sum]
    subgraph WorkStealing
      Thread1[T1: busy] 
      Thread2[T2: idle] -->|steals from T1 deque| Thread1
    end
examples.pseudocode:
FORKJOINTASK (extends RecursiveTask<T>):
  compute():
    if (size <= threshold) return directCompute()
    left  = new Task(left half);  left.fork()   // async submit
    right = right half;           right.compute() // sync on current thread
    return right + left.join()   // wait for left

WORK STEALING:
  each thread has a deque of tasks
  idle threads steal tasks from tail of busy threads' deques
  maximises CPU utilisation with minimal synchronisation

COMMON POOL:
  ForkJoinPool.commonPool() — shared, parallelism = CPU cores - 1
  parallelStream() uses commonPool internally
examples.explanation: ForkJoin is the ideal executor for divide-and-conquer: split the problem, fork (submit) one half, compute the other half on the current thread, join the forked result. Work stealing means idle threads steal pending tasks from busy threads' queues — self-balancing without explicit coordination. The common pool is shared by all parallel streams and ForkJoinTasks — avoid blocking operations in it (use a custom pool instead).
examples.realWorldUse: Java's Arrays.parallelSort() uses ForkJoin internally. Stream.parallel() uses the common ForkJoinPool. Lucene's IndexWriter uses ForkJoin for parallel segment merges. Spring Batch parallel steps use ForkJoinPool when configured with PartitionHandler. Never use ForkJoinPool.commonPool() for blocking I/O operations — it starves all parallel streams; use a separate Executors.newFixedThreadPool() instead.

problems.title: Thread-Safe Counter
problems.description: Implement a `SafeCounter` class with `increment()`, `decrement()`, `reset()`, and `getCount()` methods. It must be thread-safe — correct results under concurrent access from multiple threads. Implement using `synchronized`, then again using `AtomicInteger`. Compare both.
problems.hint1: Version 1: mark all methods `synchronized` — only one thread executes at a time.
problems.hint2: Version 2: use `AtomicInteger` field — `incrementAndGet()`, `decrementAndGet()`, `set(0)`, `get()`.
problems.hint3: Test: 4 threads each calling increment() 10,000 times — final count must be 40,000.
problems.constraints: No lost updates under concurrent access.
problems.editorial: synchronized on instance methods acquires the monitor on 'this' — only one thread inside any synchronized method at a time. AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() uses a hardware CAS instruction — faster than synchronized for simple counters because it avoids lock overhead. For complex multi-step operations, synchronized is still needed.

problems.title: Producer-Consumer With BlockingQueue
problems.description: Implement a producer-consumer system. Producer generates integers 1..N. Consumer processes each integer (prints it). Use `LinkedBlockingQueue` with capacity 5. One producer thread, two consumer threads. Producer signals consumers when done using a sentinel value (-1).
problems.hint1: `LinkedBlockingQueue<Integer>(5)` — blocks put() when full, blocks take() when empty.
problems.hint2: Use a sentinel value (-1) to signal consumer shutdown. Put one sentinel per consumer thread.
problems.hint3: Consumer loop: `while (true) { Integer v = queue.take(); if (v == -1) break; process(v); }`
problems.constraints: Queue capacity = 5 (backpressure). One sentinel per consumer.
problems.editorial: BlockingQueue.put() blocks when full (backpressure on the producer). take() blocks when empty (consumer waits for work). The sentinel pattern (-1 per consumer) is the standard shutdown mechanism. Using -1 per consumer ensures each consumer gets exactly one shutdown signal — if you put one sentinel for two consumers, one consumer might take it and the other blocks forever.

problems.title: Deadlock Detection — Show and Fix
problems.description: The given code has a deadlock: T1 locks A then B; T2 locks B then A. Demonstrate the deadlock (both threads block forever), then fix it by enforcing a consistent lock ordering. Verify the fix works.
problems.hint1: Deadlock condition: T1 acquires lockA, T2 acquires lockB, then each waits for the other.
problems.hint2: Fix: always acquire locks in the same order — both threads lock A before B.
problems.hint3: Use a tryLock with timeout to detect and recover from deadlock as an alternative fix.

testCases.output:
Task1 done
Task2Fixed done
t1 alive: false t2 alive: false

problems.constraints: No sleep() in the fix. Lock order must be consistent.
problems.editorial: Deadlock requires four conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait. Breaking any one prevents deadlock. Consistent lock ordering eliminates circular wait — the cheapest fix. Alternatively, tryLock(timeout) breaks hold-and-wait: if you can't get the second lock within a timeout, release the first and retry.

problems.title: Parallel Merge Sort
problems.description: Implement parallel merge sort using `ForkJoinPool` and `RecursiveAction`. Split arrays larger than a threshold (1000) into two halves and sort them in parallel. Merge the sorted halves. Compare sequential vs parallel timing on a 1M element array.
problems.hint1: Extend `RecursiveAction` (no return value). Override `compute()`.
problems.hint2: Base case: `array.length <= THRESHOLD` → `Arrays.sort(array, from, to)` directly.
problems.hint3: Fork left half, compute right half on current thread, join left, then merge.
problems.constraints: Threshold=1000. Arrays.sort() for base case (faster than manual sort for small arrays).
problems.editorial: RecursiveAction has no return value — it sorts in place. Fork left half async, compute right half on current thread, join left, merge. The merge step must happen after both halves are sorted — after join(). Threshold 1000 is empirically good — below this, task creation overhead exceeds the parallelism benefit.

problems.title: Async HTTP Chain With CompletableFuture
problems.description: Simulate an async microservice call chain: (1) fetch user ID → (2) fetch user profile → (3) fetch user's orders → (4) compute total order value. Each step is a simulated async call (sleep + return). Chain using `CompletableFuture` without blocking between steps.
problems.hint1: `CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchUserId())` starts the chain.
problems.hint2: Use `thenCompose` when the next step is itself async (returns a CF).
problems.hint3: Use `thenApply` when the next step is synchronous (returns a plain value).
problems.constraints: Use thenCompose for async steps — not thenApply (which would nest CFs).
problems.editorial: thenCompose is flatMap for CompletableFuture — when the next step itself is async (returns a CF), thenCompose unwraps it. thenApply wraps the result in another CF (giving CF<CF<T>>). The chain runs sequentially step by step (~90ms total = 20+30+40ms) — each step starts when the previous finishes. For independent steps, use supplyAsync in parallel and combine with allOf.

problems.title: Rate Limiter — Token Bucket
problems.description: Implement a `RateLimiter` that allows at most `N` requests per second using the token bucket algorithm. `acquire()` blocks until a token is available. Tokens are added at a fixed rate. Demonstrate with 10 requests at a rate of 5/second.
problems.hint1: Use `Semaphore(N)` where N is the burst capacity. A background thread adds tokens (releases permits) at the rate.
problems.hint2: `semaphore.acquire()` in acquire() — blocks if no tokens available.
problems.hint3: Token refill: `ScheduledExecutorService` with `scheduleAtFixedRate` — release `tokensPerSecond` permits every second.
problems.constraints: Semaphore permits cap at tokensPerSecond — don't release beyond capacity.
problems.editorial: Semaphore is a counting permit — acquire() decrements (blocks at 0), release() increments. Token bucket: semaphore starts full, acquire() consumes tokens, background task refills up to capacity every second. Cap the refill at capacity (tokensPerSecond - availablePermits()) to prevent unbounded permit accumulation during idle periods. Guava's RateLimiter uses a similar algorithm.

problems.title: Dining Philosophers
problems.description: Implement the Dining Philosophers problem: 5 philosophers sit around a table with 5 forks. Each philosopher needs both adjacent forks to eat. Implement a deadlock-free solution. Use `ReentrantLock` with consistent lock ordering (lowest fork ID first).
problems.hint1: Each fork is a `ReentrantLock`. Philosopher i needs fork[i] and fork[(i+1)%5].
problems.hint2: Always acquire the lower-numbered fork first: `int first = Math.min(left, right); int second = Math.max(left, right);`
problems.hint3: Each philosopher: think → acquire first fork → acquire second fork → eat → release both.
problems.constraints: No deadlock. No starvation in this simple version.
problems.editorial: Without ordering, philosopher 0 grabs fork 0 and waits for fork 1; philosopher 1 grabs fork 1 and waits for fork 2; ... philosopher 4 grabs fork 4 and waits for fork 0 — circular wait. The fix: always acquire the lower-numbered fork first. Philosopher 4 (needs forks 4 and 0) now acquires fork 0 first, competing directly with philosopher 0 — circular dependency broken.

problems.title: Non-Blocking Queue With CAS
problems.description: Implement a simple non-blocking (lock-free) queue using `AtomicReference` and CAS. Support `enqueue(T)` and `dequeue()` (returns null if empty). Use a singly-linked list with atomic head/tail pointers. This is the Michael-Scott queue algorithm.
problems.hint1: Node<T> has `T value` and `AtomicReference<Node<T>> next`. Head and tail are `AtomicReference<Node<T>>`.
problems.hint2: Enqueue CAS loop: read tail, try CAS(tail.next, null, newNode). If fail, advance tail.
problems.hint3: Dequeue CAS loop: read head, read head.next. If null, queue empty. CAS(head, currentHead, head.next).
problems.constraints: No locks anywhere. CAS-based linearizable queue.
problems.editorial: Michael-Scott queue: dummy sentinel node simplifies edge cases. Enqueue CAS-swaps null into the tail's next pointer. The 'help advance stale tail' pattern means a thread that sees tail pointing to a non-last node helps advance it before inserting — ensures tail is always at or one step behind the last node. ConcurrentLinkedQueue in Java uses this same algorithm.

problems.title: ForkJoin Fibonacci
problems.description: Implement Fibonacci(n) using `ForkJoinPool` and `RecursiveTask<Long>`. For n <= 10, compute directly (base case). For larger n, fork fib(n-1) and compute fib(n-2) on the current thread, then join and add. Compare with sequential recursive Fibonacci.
problems.hint1: `RecursiveTask<Long>` — override `compute()` returning `Long`.
problems.hint2: Base case: `if (n <= 10) return fibSequential(n)` — avoids excessive task creation.
problems.hint3: `ForkJoinTask<Long> f1 = new FibTask(n-1).fork(); long r2 = new FibTask(n-2).compute(); return r2 + f1.join();`
problems.constraints: Base case threshold = 10 to avoid task explosion.
problems.editorial: Without a base case threshold, fib(40) creates ~2^40 tasks — catastrophic overhead. Threshold of 10 means ~2^30 fewer tasks. The fork-join pattern: fork n-1 asynchronously, compute n-2 on current thread, join n-1. This exposes as much parallelism as available cores can use without creating excessive tasks.

problems.title: Thread-Safe LRU Cache
problems.description: Implement a thread-safe `LRUCache<K,V>` with `get(K)` and `put(K,V)`. Uses `LinkedHashMap` access-order for LRU eviction. Must be safe for concurrent access. Return null for cache misses. Constructor takes capacity.
problems.hint1: `LinkedHashMap<K,V>(cap, 0.75f, true)` with `removeEldestEntry` override for LRU.
problems.hint2: Wrap all operations in `synchronized(this)` or make the LinkedHashMap operations synchronized.
problems.hint3: get() must also call `map.get(key)` inside synchronized — LinkedHashMap is not thread-safe.

testCases.output:
A
null
D
Concurrent test passed (no exception)

problems.constraints: synchronized on get and put — LinkedHashMap is not thread-safe.
problems.editorial: LinkedHashMap access-order: get() moves the accessed entry to the tail. removeEldestEntry evicts the head (LRU) when size exceeds capacity. synchronized on both get and put prevents concurrent modification — LinkedHashMap modifies its internal linked list on every get(), which is not thread-safe. For high concurrency, use ConcurrentHashMap with a separate doubly-linked list for order tracking (more complex but avoids a global lock).

problems.title: CountDownLatch — Parallel Service Initialisation
problems.description: Simulate 5 services starting in parallel. The main thread waits until ALL services are ready before proceeding. Each service takes a random time (50-150ms) to start. Use `CountDownLatch`. Also implement a second variant where the main thread continues as soon as ANY one service is ready using `CyclicBarrier` approach.
problems.hint1: `CountDownLatch latch = new CountDownLatch(5)` — main calls `latch.await()`, each service calls `latch.countDown()`.
problems.hint2: Parallel: submit all 5 as tasks to an ExecutorService, then await the latch.
problems.hint3: For `anyOf` behaviour: use `CompletableFuture.anyOf(futures...)` — continues when first completes.

testCases.output: All services ready in ~100-150ms (parallel, not 250-750ms sequential)

problems.constraints: Total time ~= slowest service, not sum of all services.
problems.editorial: CountDownLatch: initialised with count N. countDown() decrements by 1. await() blocks until count reaches 0. Unlike join(), it decouples the waiter from the thread — any thread can call countDown(). The parallel init time equals the slowest service, not their sum — 5 services × 100ms avg = 100ms instead of 500ms. CountDownLatch cannot be reset; use CyclicBarrier for reusable barriers.

problems.title: Semaphore — Connection Pool
problems.description: Implement a `ConnectionPool` using `Semaphore` to limit concurrent database connections to `maxConnections`. `acquire()` blocks until a connection slot is available (with a timeout). `release()` frees the slot. Simulate 10 threads competing for 3 connection slots.
problems.hint1: `Semaphore semaphore = new Semaphore(maxConnections)` — permits = available connections.
problems.hint2: `semaphore.tryAcquire(timeout, TimeUnit)` — non-blocking with timeout, returns boolean.
problems.hint3: Always `semaphore.release()` in a finally block — even if the operation throws.
problems.constraints: Fair semaphore (FIFO). release() always called — no permits leaked.
problems.editorial: Semaphore(max, true): the fair=true flag ensures FIFO ordering — threads acquire in the order they called tryAcquire. Without fairness, a thread might starve if others keep arriving. 10 threads × 200ms use / 3 slots = ~3.3 rounds × 200ms = ~660ms total. 500ms timeout allows batches to complete. HikariCP connection pool uses a Semaphore for connection counting.

problems.title: Read-Write Lock — Cache With High Read Contention
problems.description: Implement a `ReadWriteCache<K,V>` using `ReentrantReadWriteLock`. Multiple threads can read concurrently (shared read lock). Only one thread can write (exclusive write lock). Demonstrate that reads don't block each other but writes block both reads and other writes.
problems.hint1: `ReentrantReadWriteLock rwLock = new ReentrantReadWriteLock()`. Read: `rwLock.readLock().lock()`. Write: `rwLock.writeLock().lock()`.
problems.hint2: get(): acquire read lock, read, release. put(): acquire write lock, write, release.
problems.hint3: Test: measure time for 8 threads all reading simultaneously — should be ~1 read time, not 8× read time.

testCases.output:
8 concurrent reads in ~50ms (expect ~50ms)
Concurrent reads ok: true

problems.constraints: Read lock allows concurrent readers. Write lock is exclusive.
problems.editorial: ReadWriteLock: multiple threads hold the read lock simultaneously — reads are parallel. The write lock is exclusive — blocks all reads and other writes. Ideal when reads dominate (cache hit rate > 80%). Downgrade: you can downgrade write lock to read lock by acquiring read, then releasing write. Cannot upgrade read to write — would cause deadlock.

problems.title: Thread Pool With Bounded Queue and Rejection
problems.description: Create a `ThreadPoolExecutor` with 2 core threads, 4 max threads, 60s keep-alive, a bounded queue of size 3. Use `CallerRunsPolicy` as the rejection handler (caller thread executes the rejected task). Submit 10 tasks and observe which thread executes each.
problems.hint1: `new ThreadPoolExecutor(2, 4, 60L, SECONDS, new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(3), new CallerRunsPolicy())`
problems.hint2: Tasks 1-2 start on core threads. Tasks 3-5 go to queue. Tasks 6-8 create extra threads (up to max 4). Task 9-10 saturate all — CallerRunsPolicy runs them on the submitter thread.
problems.hint3: Log `Thread.currentThread().getName()` in each task to see which thread runs it.
problems.constraints: CallerRunsPolicy — rejected tasks run on main thread, slowing submission naturally (backpressure).
problems.editorial: ThreadPoolExecutor decision tree: tasks 1-2 → core threads. Tasks 3-5 → queue (capacity 3). Tasks 6-8 → new threads up to max 4. Tasks 9-10 → queue full, max threads reached → CallerRunsPolicy runs on the submitting thread. CallerRunsPolicy is elegant backpressure: slows the producer naturally when the pool is saturated, without throwing exceptions. Compare with AbortPolicy (throws RejectedExecutionException) or DiscardPolicy (silently drops).

problems.title: Phaser — Multi-Phase Parallel Computation
problems.description: Use `Phaser` to coordinate a 3-phase computation: 5 workers each process data in Phase 1, then synchronise, process in Phase 2, synchronise again, then Phase 3. All workers must complete each phase before any proceeds to the next. Print phase transitions.
problems.hint1: `Phaser phaser = new Phaser(5)` — 5 registered parties. `phaser.arriveAndAwaitAdvance()` marks arrival and waits.
problems.hint2: Each worker: do phase 1 work → arriveAndAwaitAdvance() → do phase 2 work → arrive... → phase 3.
problems.hint3: `phaser.getPhase()` returns current phase number (0, 1, 2...).

testCases.output: === Phase 0 complete ... === Phase 1 complete ... === Phase 2 complete

problems.constraints: All workers must finish each phase before any starts the next.
problems.editorial: Phaser is a reusable barrier — unlike CountDownLatch (single use) it advances through phases. onAdvance() is called after all parties arrive — override to print transitions or stop the phaser. arriveAndDeregister() reduces the party count — when it reaches 0, the phaser terminates. Unlike CyclicBarrier, Phaser supports dynamic registration/deregistration of parties.

problems.title: Concurrent HashMap — Parallel Word Count
problems.description: Given a large list of sentences, count word frequencies in parallel using `ConcurrentHashMap.merge()` and a parallel stream. Compare with a sequential HashMap approach. Verify correctness and measure speedup.
problems.hint1: `ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer>` — thread-safe, no external synchronisation needed.
problems.hint2: `map.merge(word, 1, Integer::sum)` — atomic update: if absent put 1, else add 1.
problems.hint3: sentences.parallelStream().flatMap(s -> Arrays.stream(s.split('\\s+'))).forEach(w -> map.merge(w, 1, Integer::sum))`
problems.constraints: ConcurrentHashMap.merge() is atomic — safe for parallel forEach.
problems.editorial: ConcurrentHashMap.merge(key, 1, Integer::sum) is a single atomic operation — no external synchronisation needed. HashMap.merge() in a parallelStream is NOT safe (race condition). The parallel version splits the sentence list across CPU cores, each core building partial counts into the shared ConcurrentHashMap. merge() uses fine-grained segment locking internally.

problems.title: Exchanger — Two-Thread Data Swap
problems.description: Use `Exchanger<T>` to implement a pipeline where a producer thread fills a buffer and a consumer thread processes it. They exchange buffers at each step — the producer gets an empty buffer, the consumer gets a full one. Produce and consume 3 batches of 5 integers each.
problems.hint1: `Exchanger<List<Integer>> exchanger = new Exchanger<>()` — both threads call `exchange(buffer)`.
problems.hint2: Producer: fill buffer, call `buffer = exchanger.exchange(buffer)`, continue filling fresh buffer.
problems.hint3: Consumer: call `buffer = exchanger.exchange(emptyBuffer)` to receive full buffer, process it.
problems.constraints: Exchanger synchronises exactly two threads — blocks until both call exchange().
problems.editorial: Exchanger is a synchronisation point for exactly two threads. Both call exchange(v) — each gets the other's value. Used for double-buffering pipelines: producer fills buffer A, consumer processes buffer B, then they swap. This eliminates allocation overhead (reuse buffers) and naturally paces the producer to the consumer's speed.

problems.title: CompletableFuture — Timeout and Cancellation
problems.description: Implement `fetchWithTimeout(Callable<T> task, long timeoutMs)` that runs a task asynchronously and cancels it if it doesn't complete within `timeoutMs`. Return `Optional<T>` — the result if successful, empty if timed out. Use `orTimeout()` (Java 9+) or manual `completeOnTimeout()`.
problems.hint1: `CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(supplier).orTimeout(timeout, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)` — completes exceptionally on timeout.
problems.hint2: Wrap `orTimeout()` with `.exceptionally(e -> null)` then check if result is null.
problems.hint3: Or use `.completeOnTimeout(defaultValue, timeout, unit)` which completes normally with the default.
problems.constraints: Task thread continues running even after timeout (no forceful interruption in this impl).
problems.editorial: completeOnTimeout(defaultValue, timeout, unit) completes the future normally with the default value if it hasn't completed by the timeout. orTimeout() completes exceptionally with TimeoutException instead. Optional.ofNullable wraps null into empty(). Note: the background task continues executing even after timeout — for true cancellation, use Future.cancel(true) to interrupt the thread.

problems.title: Volatile vs Synchronized — Visibility Test
problems.description: Write a test that demonstrates the visibility problem: without `volatile`, a reader thread may never see a flag set by another thread (it reads a stale cached value). Fix with `volatile`. Then show that `volatile` is NOT sufficient for compound operations — demonstrate the race condition with volatile++.
problems.hint1: Without volatile: reader thread caches `running = true` in register, never checks main memory. Loop may run forever.
problems.hint2: With volatile: every read goes to main memory — reader always sees the latest value.
problems.hint3: Volatile ++ race: `volatile int x = 0; x++` is still read-modify-write — NOT atomic. Show final value < expected.
problems.constraints: Demonstrates the JMM visibility guarantee and its limitations.
problems.editorial: volatile guarantees: (1) reads always go to main memory — no stale CPU cache. (2) writes immediately go to main memory and are visible to all threads. volatile does NOT guarantee: atomicity for read-modify-write sequences. volatile++ is still three ops: read(0) → add(1) → write(1). Two threads can both read 0, both add 1, both write 1 — lost update. Use AtomicInteger for safe concurrent increment.

problems.title: Virtual Threads — Project Loom (Java 21)
problems.description: Demonstrate Java 21 Virtual Threads: create 10,000 virtual threads each doing a blocking operation (Thread.sleep). Compare with platform threads — virtual threads don't block OS threads. Show that 10,000 virtual threads complete in the time of ONE sleep, while 10,000 platform threads would require a huge pool.
problems.hint1: `Thread.ofVirtual().start(runnable)` creates a virtual thread. Or `Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()`.
problems.hint2: 10,000 virtual threads sleeping 100ms should all complete in ~100ms — the JVM multiplexes them onto carrier threads.
problems.hint3: 10,000 platform threads would require 10,000 OS threads — typically hits system limits around 10,000.
problems.constraints: Requires Java 21+. Virtual threads are JVM-managed lightweight threads.
problems.editorial: Virtual threads (Project Loom, Java 21) are JVM-managed threads multiplexed onto a small pool of OS 'carrier' threads. When a virtual thread blocks (Thread.sleep, I/O), the JVM parks it and frees the carrier thread for another virtual thread — no OS thread blocked. 10,000 virtual threads sleeping 100ms complete in ~100ms because they all sleep concurrently on a handful of carriers. This makes the thread-per-request model viable again for high-concurrency servers (Spring Boot 3.2+ enables virtual threads for HTTP requests).
